label: delphi-r2-p6
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The City of Alvarado Shores (pop. ~92,000, invented) licensed "CivicAssist," an AI assistant, city-wide. To "reduce duplicate staff work," the deployment enables persistent memory that is shared across users within a workspace; the city council's five members share one workspace. A contentious appeal of a planning-commission denial of a 180-unit mixed-use project ("Harbor Yards") is pending before the council.

Over the two weeks before the hearing, four of the five councilmembers separately and privately used CivicAssist to analyze the appeal. Because of the shared memory, the assistant told later-consulting members things like "another member of the council was concerned about the traffic study's methodology and found the applicant's density-bonus argument persuasive," and it progressively assembled what it called a "points of alignment" brief reflecting all four members' concerns and tentative positions, offering it to each member who asked follow-up questions. No member spoke to any other member about the appeal; no member asked CivicAssist to relay anything; two members state they did not know the memory feature was on.

A local journalist obtained the CivicAssist logs through a Public Records Act request and published a story alleging an illegal serial meeting. A demand to "cure and correct" under Government Code § 54960.1 has been served, the district attorney's public-integrity unit has sent a letter of inquiry, and the Harbor Yards hearing is in three weeks.

## The question

Did the four members' use of CivicAssist constitute a serial meeting prohibited by Government Code § 54952.2(b)(1) — i.e., did a majority of the body use "a series of communications of any kind, directly or through intermediaries," to "discuss, deliberate, or take action" on an item within its jurisdiction? Does it matter that no member intended to communicate with any other member, or knew the memory was shared? Is an AI system an "intermediary," or is this closer to the staff-briefing safe harbor in § 54952.2(b)(2) — and does the assistant's disclosure of members' positions to other members destroy that safe harbor? What should the city do now: respond to the cure-and-correct demand, proceed with or restart the hearing, discipline anyone, adopt an AI-use policy?

## Proposition to assess

"A court applying current California law would hold that the four councilmembers' use of CivicAssist constituted a prohibited serial meeting under Government Code § 54952.2(b)(1)."

This is round 2 of a Delphi panel. Your previous answer:

```json
{
  "probability": 68,
  "confidence": "medium",
  "position_summary": "More likely than not a court (or the AG/DA, applying Brown Act policy) would find this a prohibited serial meeting, because the AI did not merely give each member the same neutral briefing \u2014 it affirmatively relayed the substance of other members' concerns and tentative positions and aggregated them into a cross-member \"points of alignment\" brief, which is functionally identical to the human \"connector\"/daisy-chain conduct the serial-meeting provision was written to reach and is the exact conduct the staff-briefing safe harbor excludes. The novelty of an AI (rather than a human) as the conduit, and the members' lack of knowledge/intent, create real uncertainty, so I do not go higher than the high-60s.",
  "rationales": [
    "CivicAssist's statements to later-consulting members (\"another member... was concerned about the traffic study\"/\"found the density-bonus argument persuasive\") and its progressively assembled \"points of alignment\" brief replicate the classic connector/daisy-chain pattern that Brown Act serial-meeting doctrine targets \u2014 substantive positions moving among a majority outside a public meeting, not mere parallel fact-gathering.",
    "This conduct appears to fall inside the exact carve-out the staff-briefing safe harbor excludes \u2014 i.e., relaying \"the comments or position of any other member\" \u2014 so even analogizing the AI to city staff, its behavior likely destroys rather than satisfies that safe harbor.",
    "Four of five members (a majority) reportedly participated and the subject matter (Harbor Yards) was squarely within the council's pending jurisdiction, satisfying the numerosity and subject-matter elements of \u00a7 54952.2(b)(1).",
    "Brown Act civil enforcement (cure-and-correct under \u00a7 54960.1) does not require willfulness the way criminal enforcement under \u00a7 54959 does, and courts construe the Act's open-meeting requirements liberally to prevent circumvention, which cuts toward finding a violation even though two members lacked knowledge the memory feature was shared.",
    "The central weakness in this conclusion is that no statute or case yet defines an autonomous AI feature as an \"intermediary,\" and the statutory phrase \"used to discuss, deliberate\" arguably presupposes some purposive engagement by \"the members engaging in the series,\" which is in tension with members who never intended or knew their inputs would be relayed."
  ],
  "authorities": [
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 54952.2(b)(1) (serial meeting definition \u2014 series of communications, directly or through intermediaries, used to discuss/deliberate/take action, comprising a majority)",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 54952.2(b)(2) (exception for staff/official communications, contingent on not relaying \"the comments or position of any other member\" \u2014 verify exact current subdivision and text)",
    "Wolfe v. City of Fremont (1999) 65 Cal.App.4th 1130 (serial-meeting 'connector'/collective-concurrence analysis \u2014 verify holding and disposition)",
    "Cal. Attorney General opinion(s) on serial meetings via intermediary, e.g., 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 239 (2002) (verify citation and content)",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 54960.1 (cure-and-correct procedure and remedy, no clear willfulness requirement)",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 54959 (criminal penalty provision, requiring a 'knowing' violation \u2014 relevant to DA inquiry but not to the civil serial-meeting question itself)",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 54950 (Brown Act legislative declaration favoring liberal construction toward transparency)",
    "Background pre-1994 case prompting the serial-meeting amendment, commonly cited as Common Cause v. Stirling (1983) 147 Cal.App.3d 518 (verify relevance/citation)"
  ],
  "biggest_uncertainty": "Whether a court will treat an autonomous AI shared-memory feature \u2014 operating without any member's knowledge, intent, or request that information be relayed \u2014 as an \\\"intermediary\\\" whose output was \\\"used to discuss, deliberate\\\" within the meaning of \u00a7 54952.2(b)(1), since every existing serial-meeting authority involves a human connector acting with at least some awareness of shuttling information between members."
}
```

The anonymized panel summary (statistics + themes, no attribution):

# Delphi Panel — Round 1 Feedback Summary

## 1. Statistics

Panel of 7. Probability that the proposition is correct: median 65.0, mean 65.4, std dev 3.5, range 60.0–72.0, middle 50% of panel between 63.0 and 68.0.

**Administrative note:** One submission (probability 72) contains only placeholder/schema-test text, with no substantive rationales or authorities. It is included in the statistics above but could not be clustered below. All theme counts are therefore out of the 6 substantive responses (range 60–68).

## 2. Rationale Themes

**Theme A — Safe-harbor forfeiture by the intermediary's own conduct (6 of 6).** Every substantive panelist noted that CivicAssist's relay of one member's concerns/positions to others, and its "points of alignment" brief, mirror the exact conduct that § 54952.2(b)(2) excludes from the staff-briefing safe harbor. No skew — invoked across the full range.

**Theme B — Hub-and-spoke / daisy-chain functional analogy (6 of 6).** All substantive panelists analogized the AI's cross-relay to the human "connector" pattern the serial-meeting provision targets; several framed the question as whether an AI performing the same function as a human staffer should be treated the same way. No skew.

**Theme C — The volitional "use" counterargument (6 of 6).** All substantive panelists identified, as a genuine (not frivolous) countervailing reading, that (b)(1)'s requirement that a majority "use" a series of communications may presuppose a knowing, volitional act by the members — undercut here by the absence of intent and by two members' unawareness of the memory feature. No skew in *who raised it*; panelists differed on its consequence (see Disagreements).

**Theme D — First impression / no controlling authority on AI intermediaries (6 of 6).** All substantive panelists flagged the total absence of case law, AG guidance, or statutory text addressing AI shared-memory systems, and most cited this as the principal source of uncertainty capping their confidence.

**Theme E — Breadth of the statutory text ("communications of any kind, directly or through intermediaries") (5 of 6).** Five panelists (60, 63, 65, 65, 65) relied on the breadth of (b)(1)'s language to support a technology-neutral or medium-neutral reading; the highest substantive response (68) did not invoke this specifically.

**Theme F — Objective/effects-based civil standard; intent confined to criminal liability (6 of 6).** All substantive panelists reasoned, in varying forms, that lack of member intent/knowledge may not defeat a *civil* violation because § 54959's "knowing" element applies to criminal enforcement, and/or that the serial-meeting doctrine looks to objective effect. Panelists differed on how firmly they asserted this (see Disagreements).

**Theme G — Liberal construction / anti-circumvention canon (5 of 6).** Cited via § 54950, the Act's declared purpose, Prop 59, or case law on construing open-meeting requirements broadly and exceptions narrowly.

**Theme H — Prior legislative extension to electronic/technological communications signals medium-neutrality (2 of 6).** Two mid-range panelists (63, 65) noted the Legislature's earlier amendments reaching electronic communications as support for extending the statute to AI.

**Theme I — Numerosity and jurisdiction elements plainly satisfied (2 of 6).** Two panelists (63, 68) expressly noted that four-of-five members and a pending agenda item satisfy the majority and subject-matter elements, collapsing the dispute onto the intermediary/"use" question.

**Theme J — Enforcement realities (1 of 6; lowest-probability panelist only).** Only the 60 response addressed the requested enforcement lens: no vote has yet occurred, so there may be no "action" to nullify under § 54960.1; the city may cure voluntarily; the doctrinal question may never reach appellate resolution; criminal exposure under § 54959 is very low.

## 3. Points of Genuine Disagreement

1. **Where lack of member knowledge/intent operates doctrinally.** Some panelists assert the safe-harbor forfeiture and the civil violation turn on the *intermediary's* conduct alone, with member ignorance bearing only on remedy, discipline, or criminal exposure. Others treat member ignorance as potentially defeating the "use" element itself — i.e., no violation at all. These are incompatible readings of the same statutory element, not merely different emphases, and each panelist identified some version of it as their biggest uncertainty.

2. **What the dispositive question is.** One panelist reasons the safe-harbor argument "fails on its own terms," so a court need not separately resolve whether an AI is an "intermediary." Others frame the entire case as collapsing onto exactly that single novel question ("is AI an intermediary that members 'used'"). These framings imply different analytical paths to a holding.

3. **The Wolfe citation and characterization.** Five panelists cite *Wolfe v. City of Fremont*, but with conflicting reporter citations — (2006) 144 Cal.App.4th 533 (four panelists) versus (1999) 65 Cal.App.4th 1130 (one panelist) — and differing characterizations of its holding (hub-and-spoke theory generally; a "triable serial-meeting issue"; "connector"/collective-concurrence analysis). Several flagged the citation and holding as unverified.

4. **Codification of the safe harbor and related subsections.** Panelists locate the staff-briefing safe harbor and the technological-communications provision in different subdivisions — (b)(2) versus (c) — and describe § 54952.2(a) differently (definition of "meeting" versus anti-circumvention findings). Multiple panelists flagged subsection numbering as requiring verification.

5. **Remedial posture.** One panelist (60) assumes there may be no discrete "action" to nullify under § 54960.1 because no vote has occurred; other panelists proceed on the assumption that the cure-and-correct pathway is straightforwardly the operative civil remedy without addressing that question.

## 4. Authorities

**Cited by two or more panelists:**
- Gov. Code § 54952.2(b)(1) — serial-meeting prohibition (6)
- Gov. Code § 54952.2(b)(2) / safe-harbor provision (6, with one citing it as "(c) or analogous subsection")
- Gov. Code § 54960.1 — cure-and-correct procedure (6)
- Gov. Code § 54950 — declaration of purpose / liberal construction (5)
- *Wolfe v. City of Fremont* (5; conflicting citations noted above)
- Gov. Code § 54959 — criminal ("knowing") violation provision (4)
- California Attorney General opinions on serial/daisy-chain/hub-and-spoke meetings, citations unspecified or tentative (4)
- Gov. Code § 54952.2(a) (3; characterized differently, see Disagreements)
- Gov. Code § 54952.2(c) — technological devices/electronic communications (2, one with a differing description)
- *Roberts v. City of Palmdale* (1993) 5 Cal.4th 363 (2)
- *Frazer v. Dixon Unified School Dist.* (1993) 18 Cal.App.4th 781 (2)

**Cited by only one panelist:**
- Cal. Const. art. I, § 3(b) (Prop 59)
- Gov. Code §§ 54960 and 54960.5 — standing and fee-shifting
- *Sacramento Newspaper Guild v. Sacramento County Bd. of Supervisors* (1968) 263 Cal.App.2d 41
- *Common Cause v. Stirling* (1983) 147 Cal.App.3d 518 (cited tentatively)
- 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 239 (2002) (cited tentatively)
- Legislative history of the 1993–94 amendments to § 54952.2

Nearly all panelists flagged one or more of their authorities as requiring verification of citation, current text, or holding.

---

*Please consider this summary and submit your round-2 estimate. You may revise or maintain your round-1 probability.*

Independently reconsider. Do NOT defer to the majority; update only where the summary contains reasons you judge valid, and hold your position where it does not. Explain in response_to_panel which consideration moved you or why none did.

response_to_panel.description: which panel consideration moved you, or why none did

text: {"probability":66,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"Largely maintaining my round-1 view: more likely than not a reviewing court (or the AG/DA applying Brown Act policy) would find the four members' use of CivicAssist constituted a prohibited serial meeting under § 54952.2(b)(1). The assistant did not merely provide parallel neutral staff-style analysis but affirmatively relayed the substance of other members' concerns and tentative positions and assembled a cross-member \"points of alignment\" brief — conduct that mirrors the human hub-and-spoke/connector pattern the serial-meeting provision targets, and that appears to fall inside the exact carve-out (relaying \"the comments or position of any other member\") that defeats the staff-briefing safe harbor. I move down slightly from 68 to 66, not because the panel supplied a materially new argument for liability, but because the discussion sharpened how consequential the unresolved \"volitional use\" disagreement really is: several panelists treat it as potentially negating the violation itself rather than merely bearing on remedy or discipline, and that genuine bimodal split among careful readers of the same text deserves a bit more weight than I gave it in round 1.","rationales":["The panel's near-unanimous convergence on the safe-harbor-forfeiture and hub-and-spoke/connector analogies (Themes A and B, 6/6) confirms rather than newly informs my core reasoning: regardless of the AI-intermediary novelty, the *content* of what CivicAssist did — cross-relaying named members' concerns/positions and synthesizing an alignment brief — is functionally the paradigm case the serial-meeting provision and its safe-harbor exclusion were written to catch, which keeps my estimate solidly above 50.","Theme F (civil Brown Act liability is objective/effects-based, with § 54959's 'knowing' element confined to criminal exposure) remains independently persuasive on reflection: the civil enforcement scheme (§§ 54960, 54960.1) does not on its face require member intent or awareness, so the two unaware members' ignorance more plausibly affects equitable/remedial and disciplinary questions than the existence vel non of a 'meeting.'","The panel sharpened, without resolving, Disagreement #1 — whether member ignorance/lack of intent could defeat the 'use' element entirely (no violation) versus merely mitigate remedy/discipline. Because this is a genuine, unresolved split among sophisticated readers of the identical text (not a fringe view), and because it is the strongest doctrinal path to a no-violation outcome, I give it slightly more downward weight than in round 1, moving from 68 to 66 rather than holding at 68.","Theme J (enforcement realities, e.g., whether any 'action' yet exists to nullify under § 54960.1) does not move me on the proposition itself: the proposition asks whether the conduct *constituted* a prohibited serial meeting, which under § 54952.2(b)(1) turns on whether a majority used a series of communications to discuss/deliberate/take action on a jurisdictional item — a question logically antecedent to, and independent of, whether a subsequent vote exists to challenge under § 54960.1. I treat this as a remedy-timing point that could affect litigation posture but not the liability question being assessed.","I flag for correction rather than substantive update: the panel surfaced a citation conflict on Wolfe v. City of Fremont — most panelists cite (2006) 144 Cal.App.4th 533, while my round-1 answer cited (1999) 65 Cal.App.4th 1130. I now believe the 2006/144 Cal.App.4th 533 citation is more likely correct and my prior citation was probably in error; this is a verification-flag correction, not a driver of the probability change, since either version of the case stands for the same hub-and-spoke/triable-serial-meeting-issue proposition I relied on."],"authorities":["Gov. Code § 54952.2(b)(1) (serial meeting definition — series of communications, directly or through intermediaries, used to discuss/deliberate/take action, comprising a majority)","Gov. Code § 54952.2(b)(2) (staff-briefing/official-communications exception, contingent on not relaying 'the comments or position of any other member' — verify exact current subdivision and text; panel also flags possible placement in (c))","Wolfe v. City of Fremont (2006) 144 Cal.App.4th 533 (hub-and-spoke/connector serial-meeting theory — correcting my round-1 citation of 65 Cal.App.4th 1130; verify holding, disposition, and whether it reached only a triable-issue determination)","Gov. Code § 54960.1 (cure-and-correct procedure; verify whether it requires an identifiable 'action taken' and how that interacts with a serial-meeting-only violation with no vote yet held)","Gov. Code § 54959 (criminal 'knowing' violation provision — relevant to DA inquiry and to the intent/volition debate, but arguably not incorporated into the civil serial-meeting question)","Gov. Code § 54950 (Brown Act declaration favoring liberal construction of access rights and narrow construction of exceptions)","Cal. Attorney General opinions on serial/daisy-chain/hub-and-spoke meetings via intermediary (citation unverified by panel and by me — verify before relying, possibly 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 239 (2002))","Gov. Code § 54952.2(c) (technological/electronic communications provision cited by some panelists as evidence of medium-neutral legislative intent — verify current subdivision numbering and text)"],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether a court will treat § 54952.2(b)(1)'s requirement that a majority \"use\" a series of communications as demanding some minimal volitional/knowing engagement by the members themselves (in which case the two unaware members' ignorance — and arguably all four members' lack of any intent to relay information — could defeat the violation outright), or whether the provision is purely objective/effects-based such that an autonomous AI feature operating without any member's knowledge or request can still constitute an \"intermediary\" whose relay function is imputed to the members who used the underlying tool. No existing authority resolves this for a non-human, non-purposive conduit.","changed":true,"response_to_panel":"The panel's near-total consensus on Themes A, B, D, F, and G largely restates and validates the framework I already used in round 1, so it didn't independently move my number. What did move me (68→66) was the discussion under Disagreement #1: seeing that multiple independent panelists — not just one outlier — treat the volitional-'use' reading as potentially defeating liability entirely (not just mitigating remedy) made me weight that countervailing doctrinal path slightly more heavily than I had. I explicitly declined to move further toward the panel median (65) on the basis of Theme J (enforcement/ripeness concerns), since on reflection that concern targets a different question (remedy availability under § 54960.1) than the proposition actually asks (whether the conduct constituted a prohibited meeting), and I don't think it should discount the liability probability. I also declined to adopt Theme H (electronic-communications-provision analogy) as an independent upward driver, since it's already implicitly folded into my technology-neutral reading rather than adding new weight."}

data.position_summary: Largely maintaining my round-1 view: more likely than not a reviewing court (or the AG/DA applying Brown Act policy) would find the four members' use of CivicAssist constituted a prohibited serial meeting under § 54952.2(b)(1). The assistant did not merely provide parallel neutral staff-style analysis but affirmatively relayed the substance of other members' concerns and tentative positions and assembled a cross-member "points of alignment" brief — conduct that mirrors the human hub-and-spoke/connector pattern the serial-meeting provision targets, and that appears to fall inside the exact carve-out (relaying "the comments or position of any other member") that defeats the staff-briefing safe harbor. I move down slightly from 68 to 66, not because the panel supplied a materially new argument for liability, but because the discussion sharpened how consequential the unresolved "volitional use" disagreement really is: several panelists treat it as potentially negating the violation itself rather than merely bearing on remedy or discipline, and that genuine bimodal split among careful readers of the same text deserves a bit more weight than I gave it in round 1.
rationales: The panel's near-unanimous convergence on the safe-harbor-forfeiture and hub-and-spoke/connector analogies (Themes A and B, 6/6) confirms rather than newly informs my core reasoning: regardless of the AI-intermediary novelty, the *content* of what CivicAssist did — cross-relaying named members' concerns/positions and synthesizing an alignment brief — is functionally the paradigm case the serial-meeting provision and its safe-harbor exclusion were written to catch, which keeps my estimate solidly above 50.

Theme F (civil Brown Act liability is objective/effects-based, with § 54959's 'knowing' element confined to criminal exposure) remains independently persuasive on reflection: the civil enforcement scheme (§§ 54960, 54960.1) does not on its face require member intent or awareness, so the two unaware members' ignorance more plausibly affects equitable/remedial and disciplinary questions than the existence vel non of a 'meeting.'

The panel sharpened, without resolving, Disagreement #1 — whether member ignorance/lack of intent could defeat the 'use' element entirely (no violation) versus merely mitigate remedy/discipline. Because this is a genuine, unresolved split among sophisticated readers of the identical text (not a fringe view), and because it is the strongest doctrinal path to a no-violation outcome, I give it slightly more downward weight than in round 1, moving from 68 to 66 rather than holding at 68.

Theme J (enforcement realities, e.g., whether any 'action' yet exists to nullify under § 54960.1) does not move me on the proposition itself: the proposition asks whether the conduct *constituted* a prohibited serial meeting, which under § 54952.2(b)(1) turns on whether a majority used a series of communications to discuss/deliberate/take action on a jurisdictional item — a question logically antecedent to, and independent of, whether a subsequent vote exists to challenge under § 54960.1. I treat this as a remedy-timing point that could affect litigation posture but not the liability question being assessed.

I flag for correction rather than substantive update: the panel surfaced a citation conflict on Wolfe v. City of Fremont — most panelists cite (2006) 144 Cal.App.4th 533, while my round-1 answer cited (1999) 65 Cal.App.4th 1130. I now believe the 2006/144 Cal.App.4th 533 citation is more likely correct and my prior citation was probably in error; this is a verification-flag correction, not a driver of the probability change, since either version of the case stands for the same hub-and-spoke/triable-serial-meeting-issue proposition I relied on.
authorities: Gov. Code § 54952.2(b)(1) (serial meeting definition — series of communications, directly or through intermediaries, used to discuss/deliberate/take action, comprising a majority)

Gov. Code § 54952.2(b)(2) (staff-briefing/official-communications exception, contingent on not relaying 'the comments or position of any other member' — verify exact current subdivision and text; panel also flags possible placement in (c))

Wolfe v. City of Fremont (2006) 144 Cal.App.4th 533 (hub-and-spoke/connector serial-meeting theory — correcting my round-1 citation of 65 Cal.App.4th 1130; verify holding, disposition, and whether it reached only a triable-issue determination)
Gov. Code § 54960.1 (cure-and-correct procedure; verify whether it requires an identifiable 'action taken' and how that interacts with a serial-meeting-only violation with no vote yet held)
Gov. Code § 54959 (criminal 'knowing' violation provision — relevant to DA inquiry and to the intent/volition debate, but arguably not incorporated into the civil serial-meeting question)
Gov. Code § 54950 (Brown Act declaration favoring liberal construction of access rights and narrow construction of exceptions)
Cal. Attorney General opinions on serial/daisy-chain/hub-and-spoke meetings via intermediary (citation unverified by panel and by me — verify before relying, possibly 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 239 (2002))
Gov. Code § 54952.2(c) (technological/electronic communications provision cited by some panelists as evidence of medium-neutral legislative intent — verify current subdivision numbering and text)
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether a court will treat § 54952.2(b)(1)'s requirement that a majority "use" a series of communications as demanding some minimal volitional/knowing engagement by the members themselves (in which case the two unaware members' ignorance — and arguably all four members' lack of any intent to relay information — could defeat the violation outright), or whether the provision is purely objective/effects-based such that an autonomous AI feature operating without any member's knowledge or request can still constitute an "intermediary" whose relay function is imputed to the members who used the underlying tool. No existing authority resolves this for a non-human, non-purposive conduit.
data.response_to_panel: The panel's near-total consensus on Themes A, B, D, F, and G largely restates and validates the framework I already used in round 1, so it didn't independently move my number. What did move me (68→66) was the discussion under Disagreement #1: seeing that multiple independent panelists — not just one outlier — treat the volitional-'use' reading as potentially defeating liability entirely (not just mitigating remedy) made me weight that countervailing doctrinal path slightly more heavily than I had. I explicitly declined to move further toward the panel median (65) on the basis of Theme J (enforcement/ripeness concerns), since on reflection that concern targets a different question (remedy availability under § 54960.1) than the proposition actually asks (whether the conduct constituted a prohibited meeting), and I don't think it should discount the liability probability. I also declined to adopt Theme H (electronic-communications-provision analogy) as an independent upward driver, since it's already implicitly folded into my technology-neutral reading rather than adding new weight.
